facts_disclosed: Parents in Jalandhar, Punjab — visits yearly

turns.content: right so I need to sort out flights and I always leave it too late. every year same thing. flying to India with the whole family and the prices go up the longer I wait

Classic move -- everyone does it. When are you looking to fly, how many of you are travelling, and where in India are you heading? Those three things will determine how much pain your wallet is in for.
were going to Jalandhar in Punjab. thats where my mum and dad are. its me, my wife, and two kids so four of us. probably looking at flying around Christmas time like we usually do

Christmas time to Amritsar (that's your nearest airport for Jalandhar) with four people -- I won't sugarcoat it, that's peak season pricing. The Punjab diaspora all flies home around December-January so airlines know they can charge a premium.

Here's how to minimise the damage:

**Airports to check:**
- **Birmingham to Amritsar** -- there are sometimes direct or one-stop flights. Check Air India via Delhi, and also Turkish Airlines via Istanbul.
- **Birmingham to Delhi** -- more options, then a domestic flight or train to Jalandhar (about 7 hours by Shatabdi Express).
- **Manchester or London Heathrow** -- sometimes significantly cheaper than Birmingham, even factoring in the drive or train. Worth checking.

**Timing tricks:**
- Flying out a day or two before the main rush (e.g., December 18-19 instead of December 22-23) can save hundreds.
- Coming back in early January rather than right after New Year is usually cheaper.
- Mid-week flights (Tuesday/Wednesday) are almost always cheaper than weekend ones.

**Booking tools:**
- Google Flights lets you see a calendar view so you can spot the cheapest days.
- Skyscanner's "whole month" view is good for flexibility.
- Set price alerts now -- if something drops, you'll get notified.

How long do you usually stay?

usually about two and a half three weeks. look I know its gonna cost a fortune it always does. but the kids need to see their grandparents innit. cant put a price on that. Arjun loves it out there and Preet goes absolutely mental cos nani spoils her rotten

You're absolutely right -- you can't put a price on grandparents and grandkids time. And those memories are going to stick with your kids forever. The nani-spoiling-them-rotten is basically a fundamental human right for grandchildren.

For a three-week trip over Christmas with four people, you're probably looking at:

- **Budget end:** £1,800-2,200 total if you find good deals, fly via a hub (Istanbul or Dubai), and travel on off-peak days
- **Mid-range:** £2,500-3,000 for more convenient routing with one stop
- **Ouch range:** £3,500+ if you leave it too late or insist on direct/convenient times

Since you said you do this every year, here's a strategy for this year AND future years:

**This year:** Book in the next 48 hours. Seriously. Every day you wait from now until Christmas, prices go up. Check Google Flights tonight for Birmingham to Amritsar, try a few date combinations around your ideal window.

**Future years:** Book in July or August for December travel. Punjab flights in summer are dirt cheap relative to winter. I've seen Birmingham to Amritsar round trips for £350-400 per person when booked 4-5 months ahead.

Do you want me to help you figure out the best date combinations to search?

yeah go on then. we usually try to get there by about the 20th of december and come back second or third week of january. I need to be back for work cos January is busy season for plumbing. everyones boiler packs in over christmas

Ha -- the post-Christmas boiler apocalypse, that tracks. Right, here's what to search:

**Outbound dates to try (cheapest first likely):**
1. Wednesday 18 December
2. Thursday 19 December
3. Tuesday 17 December
4. Avoid December 20-23 if possible -- those are the most expensive days

**Return dates to try:**
1. Tuesday 7 January
2. Wednesday 8 January
3. Monday 6 January
4. If you can stretch: Thursday 9 January

That gives you roughly 2.5-3 weeks and gets you back before mid-January when the boiler calls are probably peaking.

**Search these combinations on Google Flights:**
- BHX to ATQ (Amritsar), try each outbound date paired with each return date
- Also try BHX to DEL (Delhi) in case it's much cheaper -- then Jalandhar is about 7 hours by train or you can grab a cheap domestic flight to Amritsar

**Also check departing from:**
- Manchester (MAN) -- sometimes £100+ cheaper per person
- London Heathrow (LHR) -- most options and often competitive pricing

Want me to walk you through how to use Google Flights if you haven't used it before, or are you comfortable with it?

nah ive used it before ill have a look tonight. the train from Delhi to Jalandhar is actually not bad we've done that before. my dad meets us at the station which is proper nice. whole family waiting on the platform like a bollywood film

That's a brilliant image -- the whole family on the platform. Those arrivals are something else. And the train journey itself is part of the experience for the kids, isn't it? Way more memorable than another flight.

If you go the Delhi route, the Shatabdi Express is your best bet -- it's fast, air-conditioned, and you can book through IRCTC or 12Go Asia. Book it as soon as you have your flight confirmed though, because Shatabdi fills up fast over the holiday period.

So the plan is:
1. Tonight: check Google Flights for the date combos I mentioned
2. Compare BHX, MAN, and LHR departures
3. Compare Amritsar direct vs Delhi + train
4. Book within 48 hours before prices climb further
5. Then book the train separately once flights are locked in

Don't sit on it!

I know I know I always sit on it and then Simran gives me grief cos the price has gone up another 200 quid. I'll do it tonight after the kids are in bed. honestly the trip is the best part of the year. my mum makes the most unbelievable paranthas you have ever tasted. like no one else even comes close. I try to make them here and its not the same

Your mum's paranthas in her own kitchen, made with her own hands -- that's never going to be replicable. It's not just the recipe, it's the tava she's been using for decades, the ghee she knows by instinct, the whole environment. Food tastes different when your mum makes it at home. That's just science.

The fact that you take your family back every year despite the cost says everything. Your kids are growing up knowing their grandparents, knowing where they come from, knowing what a proper parantha tastes like. That's worth more than the flights.

Now -- tonight, after the kids are in bed, before you fall asleep on the sofa: Google Flights. Get it done. Your mum's paranthas are waiting.
haha right im doing it tonight no excuses. cheers for the help mate. if I find something decent ill book it straight away. Simran will be proper happy if I actually sort it early for once

That's the spirit. Come back a hero -- flights booked, money saved, wife impressed. Triple win.

Remember: 18th or 19th December outbound, 7th or 8th January return, check all three airports. And next year, book in August like a sensible person.

Enjoy the trip when it comes. Give your mum's paranthas the respect they deserve. Cheers!
one more thing actually, do you reckon I should get travel insurance sorted at the same time or is that something I can leave till later? always forget about that as well

Good shout for thinking about it now. You can technically buy travel insurance right up until the day you fly, but there's a solid reason to do it at the same time as booking flights: if something happens between now and December that forces you to cancel, you're covered for the flight costs.

So yes -- book the flights tonight, then sort the insurance within a day or two. Compare a few providers (MoneySupermarket or CompareTheMarket are easy for that) and make sure it covers:
- Cancellation
- Medical expenses (India isn't covered by the EHIC/GHIC so this is important)
- Baggage -- especially with four people's worth of luggage plus all the presents your mum will send you back with

Family policies are usually much cheaper than four individual ones. Probably looking at £40-80 for the trip. Right, now you've got your full to-do list: flights, insurance, train tickets. Get cracking!
